schools.summary: Gill: the following section, relating to the consecration of Aaron and his sons, was delivered, and now Moses is bidden to perform what had been before appointed; the witnesses note God speaking to Moses to begin the consecration of Aaron and his sons — the opening of the rite, the carrying out of what God had before commanded (God now bidding the consecration be done: 'the LORD spake unto Moses, saying'; the connexion, that the law of the consecration had been before appointed, and now God commands its performance; that this section relating to the consecration of Aaron and his sons begins here, the actual doing of what was ordered in the mount; the order of God's work, that he first gives the pattern and then commands the doing, the word going before the deed; the meaning the commentators draw, that the priesthood and its consecration are wholly of God's appointment, none taking the honour to himself but he that is called of God; that the way of approach to God is set up by his own word).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses
saying
the LORD speaking to Moses to begin the consecration of Aaron and his sons
the priesthood and its consecration wholly of God's appointment, none taking the honour but he that is called of God

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was to order Aaron and his sons to appear at the door of the tabernacle, with the garments, the anointing oil, the bullock for the sin offering, the two rams, and the basket of unleavened bread; the witnesses note God charging Moses to take Aaron and his sons, the garments, the anointing oil, a bullock for the sin offering, two rams, and a basket of unleavened bread — the gathering of all that was needed for the consecration, exactly as appointed (everything brought ready for the rite: 'Take Aaron and his sons with him, and the garments, and the anointing oil, and a bullock for the sin offering, and two rams, and a basket of unleavened bread'; the persons, 'Aaron and his sons,' to be consecrated; the garments and oil, 'the garments, and the anointing oil,' the holy vestments and the oil of consecration; the sacrifices, 'a bullock for the sin offering, and two rams,' the sin offering, the burnt offering, and the ram of consecration; the bread, 'a basket of unleavened bread,' the meat offering of the rite; that all these were exactly what God had appointed in Exodus 29, nothing wanting, nothing added; the type the commentators draw, that the consecration of Aaron and his sons, by washing, robing, anointing, and sacrifice, shadows the consecrating of Christ the great High Priest and of believers as priests unto God; that all is made ready for God's appointed service).

themes: Take Aaron and his sons with him, and the garments, and the anointing oil, and a bullock for the sin offering
and two rams, and a basket of unleavened bread
the gathering of all needed for the consecration, exactly as appointed in Exodus 29
the consecration by washing, robing, anointing, and sacrifice shadowing the consecrating of Christ the great High Priest and of believers

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was to gather all the congregation together, that is, the heads of the tribes and the elders of the people, as Aben Ezra interprets it, the whole being represented by them; the witnesses note God commanding Moses to gather all the congregation to the door of the tabernacle — the public gathering of the people to witness the consecration of their priests (the rite done openly before all: 'gather thou all the congregation together unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation'; the gathering, 'gather... all the congregation,' that is, the heads of the tribes and the elders of the people, who represented the whole; the place, 'unto the door of the tabernacle,' at the public entrance, where all might see; the reason, that the priests were to be consecrated publicly, before the people, that all might know them to be set apart of God to minister for them; the meaning the commentators draw, that the priest is for the people, ordained to act for men in things pertaining to God, and so consecrated in their sight; that the people had an interest in the priesthood, the priest standing for them; that God's ordinances are done openly, not in a corner).

themes: And gather thou all the congregation together
unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
all the congregation gathered to witness the consecration of their priests, done publicly
the priest ordained to act for men in things pertaining to God, the people having an interest in the priesthood

schools.summary: Gill: Moses convened Aaron and his sons and the heads of the people at the door of the tabernacle, doing as the LORD commanded him; the witnesses note Moses doing as the LORD commanded, and the assembly gathered to the door of the tabernacle — the exact obedience of Moses and the gathering of the people (all done as God ordered: 'Moses did as the LORD commanded him; and the assembly was gathered together unto the door of the tabernacle'; the obedience, 'Moses did as the LORD commanded him,' that Moses convened Aaron and his sons, and the heads of the people, doing precisely as appointed; the gathering, 'the assembly was gathered together unto the door,' that the people came together at the appointed place; the refrain of exact obedience, that Moses did all as the LORD commanded, a phrase often repeated in this chapter; the meaning the commentators draw, that God's servant does exactly as God commands, neither more nor less, the rule of all true service; that obedience is the mark of God's faithful minister; that what is done in God's service is to be done by his direction).

themes: And Moses did as the LORD commanded him
and the assembly was gathered together unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
Moses doing exactly as the LORD commanded, the assembly gathered at the door
God's servant doing precisely as God commands, neither more nor less, the rule of all true service, obedience the mark of his faithful minister

schools.summary: Gill: Moses, having convened them, opened to them the reason of their being called together, which was not of his own will, but the thing which the LORD commanded to be done; the witnesses note Moses declaring to the congregation that the consecration was the thing which the LORD commanded to be done — the owning of the divine warrant, that all was by God's command, not man's will (what is done resting on God's authority: 'Moses said unto the congregation, This is the thing which the LORD commanded to be done'; the declaration, 'This is the thing which the LORD commanded,' that the rite they were to see was not of Moses' own will, but the thing which the LORD commanded; that Moses claimed no honour or invention for himself, but laid all on the divine warrant; the meaning the commentators draw, that in God's worship and ordinances all must rest on his command, not on human authority or invention; that the consecration of the priesthood was wholly of God's institution, that the people might receive it as from him; that what God commands is to be done, and done because he commands it; that the minister of God acts not in his own name but in God's).

themes: And Moses said unto the congregation
This is the thing which the LORD commanded to be done
Moses owning the divine warrant, that the consecration was the LORD's command, not his own will
in God's worship all must rest on his command, not human invention; the minister acting not in his own name but in God's

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Moses brought Aaron and his sons, and washed them with water, at the laver in the court of the tabernacle; the witnesses note Moses bringing Aaron and his sons to the laver and washing them with water — the first act of the consecration, the washing, the cleansing before service (the priests first washed: 'Moses brought Aaron and his sons, and washed them with water'; the bringing, 'brought Aaron and his sons,' to the laver which was in the court of the tabernacle; the washing, 'washed them with water,' that, before they were clothed or anointed, they were washed with water, the first act of consecration, signifying cleansing; the great type the commentators draw, that this washing shadows the cleansing needful before any may serve God, the washing of regeneration and of the word, and that Christ, our High Priest, was holy and needed no washing, but his people must be washed; that none may approach God's service unwashed and unclean; that the washing went before the robing and anointing, cleansing first; that we must be washed to be priests unto God).

themes: And Moses brought Aaron and his sons
and washed them with water
the first act of consecration, the priests washed with water before being clothed or anointed
a type of the washing of regeneration and of the word, none may approach God's service unwashed and unclean

schools.summary: Gill: he put on Aaron the embroidered coat of fine linen, which was next his flesh, and all the garments in order, the girdle, the robe, the ephod, and the curious girdle of the ephod; the witnesses note Moses clothing Aaron with the coat, the girdle, the robe, the ephod, and the curious girdle, binding it to him — the robing of the high priest in his holy garments after the washing (being washed, he is clothed for his office: 'he put upon him the coat, and girded him with the girdle, and clothed him with the robe, and put the ephod upon him... and bound it unto him'; the order, that the washing went before, and now the robing, the clean priest clothed; the garments, 'the coat... the girdle... the robe... the ephod... the curious girdle of the ephod,' each put on in order, just in its place, the holy and beautiful vestments before appointed; that the coat, of fine linen, was next the flesh, the robe over it, the ephod over all, and the curious girdle binding the ephod fast; the type the commentators draw, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ, our High Priest, and his people are clothed for God's service; that, washed and then robed, the priest was fitted to draw near; that we must be clothed with righteousness to serve God).

themes: he put upon him the coat, and girded him with the girdle, and clothed him with the robe, and put the ephod upon him
and he girded him with the curious girdle of the ephod, and bound it unto him therewith
Aaron, being washed, then clothed with the coat, girdle, robe, ephod, and curious girdle in order
a type of the righteousness and graces with which Christ our High Priest and his people are clothed for God's service

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he put the breastplate upon him, and also put in the breastplate the Urim and the Thummim; the witnesses note Moses putting the breastplate on Aaron and placing in it the Urim and the Thummim — the setting of the breastplate, with the oracle of lights and perfections, on the high priest (the breastplate of judgment, with the Urim and Thummim, set on him: 'he put the breastplate upon him: also he put in the breastplate the Urim and the Thummim'; the breastplate, 'the breastplate upon him,' made of the same materials as the ephod, put upon it and fastened to it, bearing the names of the tribes on the high priest's heart; the Urim and Thummim, 'the Urim and the Thummim,' the Lights and Perfections, put in the breastplate, the means by which God gave answer and direction to his people through the high priest; the great type the commentators draw, that the Urim and Thummim, the lights and perfections, shadow Christ, in whom are all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, and by whom alone we have light and direction from God; that the high priest bore both the names of the people, on the breastplate, and the oracle of God, the Urim, to present the people to God and to bring God's answer to them; that in Christ we have both our acceptance and our light).

debates.question: What do the Urim and Thummim in the breastplate shadow?

sides.position: Christ, in whom are all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3) — the 'Lights and Perfections' by whom alone we have light and direction from God; the high priest bearing both the names of the people and the oracle of God, in Christ both our acceptance and our light

themes: And he put the breastplate upon him
also he put in the breastplate the Urim and the Thummim
the breastplate set on Aaron, with the Urim and Thummim (lights and perfections), the oracle of God
a type of Christ, in whom are all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge; the high priest bearing both the people's names and God's oracle

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: he put the mitre on his head, and on it the golden plate, the holy crown, engraved Holiness to the LORD; the witnesses note Moses setting the mitre on Aaron's head and the golden plate, the holy crown, on its forefront, as the LORD commanded — the crowning of the high priest with the mitre and the plate of holiness (the priest crowned with holiness: 'he put the mitre upon his head; also upon the mitre, even upon his forefront, did he put the golden plate, the holy crown; as the LORD commanded Moses'; the mitre, 'the mitre upon his head,' the linen turban, a wrap of considerable length about his head; the golden plate, 'the golden plate, the holy crown,' set upon his forefront, the golden plate engraved Holiness to the LORD, the high priest's crown; the refrain, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' that all was done exactly as appointed; the great type the commentators draw, that the golden crown of holiness shadows Christ, our High Priest, who is holiness itself and, bearing the iniquity of the holy things, makes the worship and the worshippers accepted; that holiness becomes God's house and his priests, the high priest bearing it as his crown).

themes: he put the mitre upon his head... did he put the golden plate, the holy crown
as the LORD commanded Moses
the mitre and the golden crown of holiness ('Holiness to the LORD') set on Aaron's head
a type of Christ, who is holiness itself and, bearing the iniquity of the holy things, makes the worship and worshippers accepted

schools.summary: Gill: Moses took the anointing oil, which Bezaleel had made by the directions Moses had received from the Lord, and now anointed the tabernacle and all that was in it; the witnesses note Moses taking the anointing oil and anointing the tabernacle and all that was in it, to sanctify them — the anointing of the dwelling and its furniture, the hallowing of the place of worship (the tabernacle and all its vessels sanctified by the oil: 'Moses took the anointing oil, and anointed the tabernacle and all that was therein, and sanctified them'; the oil, 'the anointing oil,' the holy oil which Bezaleel had made by God's direction, set apart for this use; the anointing, 'anointed the tabernacle and all that was therein,' that the dwelling and all its holy furniture were anointed with the oil; the end, 'and sanctified them,' that the anointing set them apart as holy, consecrated to God's service; the meaning the commentators draw, that the oil, the type of the Holy Spirit, sanctified the place and its vessels, as the Spirit sanctifies all that is God's; that what is consecrated to God is set apart by the anointing of the Spirit; the type, that the church and all its ordinances are sanctified by the Spirit).

themes: Moses took the anointing oil, and anointed the tabernacle and all that was therein
and sanctified them
Moses anointing the tabernacle and all its furniture with the holy oil, to sanctify them
the oil, the type of the Holy Spirit, sanctifying the place and its vessels; the church and its ordinances sanctified by the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: he sprinkled the anointing oil upon the altar of burnt offering seven times, and anointed the altar and all its vessels, the laver and its foot, to sanctify them; the witnesses note Moses sprinkling the oil on the altar seven times and anointing the altar, its vessels, and the laver, to sanctify them — the sevenfold sprinkling of the altar, the full and perfect consecration (the altar sanctified by a sevenfold anointing: 'he sprinkled thereof upon the altar seven times, and anointed the altar and all his vessels, both the laver and his foot, to sanctify them'; the sprinkling, 'sprinkled thereof upon the altar seven times,' that the altar of burnt offering was sprinkled with the oil seven times, the seven being the number of completeness; the anointing, 'anointed the altar and all his vessels, both the laver and his foot,' that the altar, its vessels, and the laver were all anointed; the end, 'to sanctify them,' that all was set apart as holy; the meaning the commentators draw, that the sevenfold sprinkling marks a full and perfect consecration, the altar, the place of sacrifice, wholly hallowed, that the offerings on it might be accepted; that the altar must be sanctified before the sacrifices are offered; the type, that Christ, the altar, is perfectly hallowed, sanctifying all our offerings).

themes: he sprinkled thereof upon the altar seven times, and anointed the altar and all his vessels
both the laver and his foot, to sanctify them
Moses sprinkling the altar seven times and anointing it and the laver, to sanctify them
the sevenfold sprinkling marking a full and perfect consecration; a type of Christ, the altar, perfectly hallowed, sanctifying all our offerings

schools.summary: Gill: he poured the anointing oil upon Aaron's head, which ran down to his beard and to the collar of his coat, the robe of the ephod; Jarchi says it was first poured on his head and then between his eyebrows; the witnesses note Moses pouring the anointing oil on Aaron's head, to sanctify him — the anointing of the high priest, the pouring of the holy oil, the type of the Spirit (washed, robed, and crowned, he is now anointed: 'he poured of the anointing oil upon Aaron's head, and anointed him, to sanctify him'; the pouring, 'poured of the anointing oil upon Aaron's head,' that the oil was poured plentifully, running down to his beard, and to the collar of his coat, as in the psalm; the end, 'to sanctify him,' that the anointing set Aaron apart as the high priest, consecrated to God; the great type the commentators draw, that the anointing oil shadows the Holy Spirit, with which Christ, the Anointed One, Messiah, Christ, was anointed without measure, and his people anointed in him; that the high priest, like the holy place, was sanctified by the anointing, the same oil hallowing both the place and the priest; that none serves God aright but by the anointing of the Spirit).

debates.question: What does the anointing oil poured on Aaron's head typify?

sides.position: The Holy Spirit — with which Christ, the Anointed One (Messiah, Christ), was anointed without measure, and his people anointed in him; the high priest sanctified by the anointing, none serving God aright but by the anointing of the Spirit

themes: he poured of the anointing oil upon Aaron's head, and anointed him
to sanctify him
the anointing oil poured plentifully on Aaron's head, to sanctify him
the great type of the Holy Spirit, with which Christ the Anointed One was anointed without measure, none serving God aright but by the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: Moses brought Aaron's four sons, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, to the door of the tabernacle, and clothed them with coats, girdles, and bonnets, as the LORD commanded; the witnesses note Moses bringing Aaron's sons and clothing them with coats, girdles, and bonnets, as the LORD commanded — the robing of the common priests, the sons clothed after the high priest (the sons too clothed for their office: 'Moses brought Aaron's sons, and put coats upon them, and girded them with girdles, and put bonnets upon them; as the LORD commanded Moses'; the bringing, 'brought Aaron's sons,' the four sons, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, to be consecrated with their father; the clothing, 'put coats upon them, and girded them... and put bonnets upon them,' the plainer garments of the common priests, the coats, girdles, and bonnets, not the high priest's full vestments; the refrain, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' that all was done exactly as appointed; the type the commentators draw, that as the sons were clothed and consecrated with the father, so all believers, the spiritual priesthood, are clothed and consecrated in Christ, their great High Priest; that all God's priests, high and common, are clothed for his service).

themes: Moses brought Aaron's sons, and put coats upon them, and girded them with girdles, and put bonnets upon them
as the LORD commanded Moses
Aaron's sons brought and clothed with coats, girdles, and bonnets, the common priests robed after the high priest
a type of all believers, the spiritual priesthood, clothed and consecrated in Christ their great High Priest

schools.summary: Gill: he brought the bullock for the sin offering to the court of the tabernacle, to the altar of burnt offering, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head; the witnesses note Moses bringing the bullock for the sin offering, and Aaron and his sons laying their hands on its head — the presenting of the sin offering, the priests identifying with the victim (they lay their hands on the bullock that was to die for them: 'he brought the bullock for the sin offering: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the bullock'; the bringing, 'brought the bullock for the sin offering,' to the altar, the first of the consecration sacrifices; the laying on of hands, 'Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head,' that, by this act, they identified themselves with the victim and transferred as it were their guilt to it, the bullock standing in their stead; the great type the commentators draw, that the laying on of hands shadows the transfer of guilt to a substitute, fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD hath laid the iniquity of us all; that even the priests, before they could serve, must have their own sin atoned by a substitute; that none comes to God but by a sin-bearing sacrifice).

themes: he brought the bullock for the sin offering: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the bullock
for the sin offering
the priests laying their hands on the bullock, identifying with the victim and transferring their guilt
a type of the transfer of guilt to a substitute, fulfilled in Christ; even the priests must have their own sin atoned by a substitute

schools.summary: Gill: not Aaron nor his sons, who were not yet fully consecrated, but Moses slew it, and took the blood and put it on the horns of the altar; the witnesses note Moses slaying the bullock, putting the blood on the horns of the altar, purifying and sanctifying it to make reconciliation — the cleansing of the altar by the atoning blood, that it might be fit for the offerings (the blood purifying the altar and making reconciliation: 'Moses took the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar... and purified the altar, and poured the blood at the bottom... and sanctified it, to make reconciliation upon it'; the slaying, 'he slew it,' that Moses, the priests not yet consecrated, slew the victim; the blood on the horns, 'put it upon the horns of the altar... with his finger,' applied to the chief points of the altar; the purifying, 'purified the altar... and sanctified it,' that the altar itself was cleansed and hallowed by the blood; the end, 'to make reconciliation upon it,' that the altar was reconciled and made fit, that the offerings on it might be accepted; the great truth the commentators draw, that even the altar must be cleansed by atoning blood, and that all is reconciled and sanctified by blood; the type, that the blood of Christ cleanses and reconciles, having made peace through the blood of his cross).

themes: Moses took the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar round about with his finger, and purified the altar
and sanctified it, to make reconciliation upon it
the bullock's blood put on the horns of the altar, purifying and sanctifying it to make reconciliation
even the altar cleansed by atoning blood; a type of the blood of Christ, who cleanses and reconciles, having made peace through the blood of his cross

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: he took all the fat, in accordance with the law of the sin offering, and burned it upon the altar; the witnesses note Moses taking the fat upon the inwards, the caul above the liver, the two kidneys and their fat, and burning them upon the altar — the burning of the fat and inward parts of the sin offering to God (the fat, the best, burnt to God: 'he took all the fat that was upon the inwards, and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and their fat, and Moses burned it upon the altar'; the parts, 'all the fat... the caul above the liver... the two kidneys, and their fat,' the fat and choice inward parts, as in the peace offering; the burning, 'Moses burned it upon the altar,' that these were burnt to God, his portion; that, even in the sin offering, the fat was burnt to God on the altar, while the body was burnt without the camp; the meaning before given, that the fat, the best, given to God signifies the best and the inward devoted to him, and that the fat is the LORD's; that God claims the best even of the sin offering).

themes: he took all the fat that was upon the inwards, and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and their fat
and Moses burned it upon the altar
the fat, caul, and kidneys of the sin offering burnt upon the altar, God's portion
even in the sin offering the fat burnt to God while the body was burnt without the camp; the fat, the best, devoted to him

schools.summary: Gill: the bullock, with its hide, flesh, and dung, was burnt with fire without the camp, as the LORD had commanded; the witnesses note Moses burning the body of the bullock, its hide, flesh, and dung, with fire without the camp — the burning of the sin offering's body outside the camp, the sin borne away (the body burnt without the camp, the great type of Christ suffering without the gate: 'the bullock, and his hide, his flesh, and his dung, he burnt with fire without the camp; as the LORD commanded Moses'; the parts, 'the bullock, and his hide, his flesh, and his dung,' the whole body, all but the fat and inwards burnt on the altar; the place, 'burnt with fire without the camp,' not on the altar, but carried outside the camp and there consumed; the great type the commentators draw, that as the bodies of the sin offerings were burnt without the camp, so Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate, bearing our sin and reproach; that the sin-bearer is cast out, that the people may be cleansed; the refrain, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' exact obedience).

debates.question: What does burning the sin offering's body 'without the camp' typify?

sides.position: Christ suffering without the gate (Hebrews 13:11-12) — as the bodies of the sin offerings were burnt without the camp, so Jesus, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate, bearing our sin and reproach, the sin-bearer cast out that the people may be cleansed

themes: the bullock, and his hide, his flesh, and his dung, he burnt with fire without the camp
as the LORD commanded Moses
the body of the bullock burnt with fire without the camp, the sin borne away
the great type of Christ suffering without the gate, bearing our sin and reproach to sanctify the people

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: he brought the ram, after the sin offering, for the burnt offering, the first of the two rams; the witnesses note Moses bringing the ram for the burnt offering, and Aaron and his sons laying their hands on its head — the presenting of the burnt offering, the priests again identifying with the victim (they lay their hands on the ram of the burnt offering: 'he brought the ram for the burnt offering: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the ram'; the victim, 'the ram for the burnt offering,' the first of the two rams, now for a burnt offering, following the sin offering; the laying on of hands, 'Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head,' again identifying themselves with the victim, as with the bullock; the difference of this offering, that the sin offering atoned for guilt, but the burnt offering, wholly consumed, signified entire devotion and consecration to God; the type the commentators draw, that the burnt offering shadows Christ's entire offering of himself to God, a sweet savour, and the believer's whole devotion; that, sin first atoned, the priest is wholly given up to God).

themes: he brought the ram for the burnt offering: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the ram
for the burnt offering
the ram for the burnt offering brought, the priests identifying with the victim
the burnt offering signifying entire devotion to God, a type of Christ's whole offering, sin first atoned and then wholly given up to God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Moses killed the ram and sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about; the witnesses note Moses killing the ram of the burnt offering and sprinkling its blood round the altar — the slaying of the burnt offering and the casting of its blood about the altar (the blood dashed round the altar: 'he killed it; and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about'; the slaying, 'he killed it,' that Moses, the priests not yet fully consecrated, slew the victim; the blood, 'sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about,' that the blood was cast all about the altar, presented to God; the difference from the sin offering's blood, put on the horns, that here the blood of the burnt offering was simply dashed round the altar; that the blood, the life, was thus offered to God all about his altar, the whole devoted; the type, that the blood of the burnt offering shadows the blood of Christ, who offered himself wholly to God, his blood the ground of acceptance; that the offering is presented to God in its blood).

themes: And he killed it
and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about
Moses killing the ram of the burnt offering and dashing its blood round about the altar
a type of the blood of Christ, who offered himself wholly to God, his blood the ground of acceptance

schools.summary: Gill: he cut off its head and quartered it, and Moses burnt the head, the pieces, and the fat upon the altar; the witnesses note Moses cutting the ram into pieces and burning the head, the pieces, and the fat — the dividing and burning of the burnt offering, every part laid on the fire (the whole, cut in pieces, burnt to God: 'he cut the ram into pieces; and Moses burnt the head, and the pieces, and the fat'; the cutting, 'cut the ram into pieces,' that the head was cut off and the body quartered, divided for the altar; the burning, 'burnt the head, and the pieces, and the fat,' that every part, head, pieces, and fat, was laid on the fire and burnt to God; the meaning before given, that the whole victim, divided and laid on the fire, signifies the entire devotion of the offering, nothing kept back; that the burnt offering was wholly consumed, the mark of complete consecration; the type, that Christ offered himself wholly to God, every part devoted).

themes: And he cut the ram into pieces
and Moses burnt the head, and the pieces, and the fat
the ram cut into pieces and the head, pieces, and fat all burnt to God
signifying the entire devotion of the offering, nothing kept back; a type of Christ who offered himself wholly to God, every part devoted

schools.summary: Gill: he washed the inwards and the legs in water, the one taken out, the other cut off, and Moses burnt the whole ram upon the altar, a burnt sacrifice of a sweet savour; the witnesses note Moses washing the inwards and legs and burning the whole ram on the altar, a burnt sacrifice of a sweet savour to the LORD — the washing and wholly burning of the burnt offering, well-pleasing to God (the whole, made clean, burnt to God for a sweet savour: 'he washed the inwards and the legs in water; and Moses burnt the whole ram upon the altar... a sweet savour... as the LORD commanded Moses'; the washing, 'washed the inwards and the legs in water,' that the inner parts and legs were washed clean, that nothing unclean be offered; the burning, 'Moses burnt the whole ram upon the altar,' that the whole was wholly consumed to God; the acceptance, 'a burnt sacrifice for a sweet savour,' well-pleasing and accepted; the refrain, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' exact obedience; the great type before given, that the whole burnt offering, washed and wholly consumed, a sweet savour, shadows Christ's offering of himself, a sweetsmelling savour, pure within and wholly devoted; that what is wholly given to God, pure within, is a sweet savour).

themes: he washed the inwards and the legs in water; and Moses burnt the whole ram upon the altar: it was a burnt sacrifice for a sweet savour
as the LORD commanded Moses
the inwards washed clean and the whole ram burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD
the washing signifying purity within, the whole burning entire devotion; a type of Christ's offering, 'a sweetsmelling savour'

schools.summary: Gill: the other ram, the ram of consecration, or filling, or fulnesses, because, as Jarchi says, these filled and perfected the priests in their office; the witnesses note Moses bringing the other ram, the ram of consecration, and Aaron and his sons laying their hands on its head — the presenting of the ram of consecration, the chief offering of the rite (the ram of filling brought: 'he brought the other ram, the ram of consecration: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the ram'; the victim, 'the other ram, the ram of consecration,' in the Hebrew the ram of filling or fulnesses, so called because by it the priests were filled and perfected in their office; the laying on of hands, 'Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head,' again identifying themselves with the victim, as before; the special place of this offering, that it was the proper consecration sacrifice, by which the priests were installed and their hands filled; the type the commentators draw, that the ram of consecration, the ram of filling, shadows Christ, who fills his people's hands with himself and his gifts, by whom they are consecrated to their priesthood; that none is consecrated to God but through the appointed sacrifice).

themes: And he brought the other ram, the ram of consecration: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the ram
the ram of consecration
the ram of consecration ('the ram of filling') brought, by which the priests were filled and perfected in their office
a type of Christ, who fills his people's hands with himself and his gifts, by whom they are consecrated to their priesthood

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Moses took of the blood and put it upon the tip of Aaron's right ear, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot; the witnesses note Moses slaying the ram and putting the blood on Aaron's right ear, right thumb, and right great toe — the touching of the high priest with blood at ear, hand, and foot, the whole man consecrated (the blood claiming all his powers for God: 'Moses took of the blood of it, and put it upon the tip of Aaron's right ear, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot'; the three places, the tip of the right ear, the thumb of the right hand, the great toe of the right foot, the chief parts of hearing, doing, and walking; the great meaning the commentators draw, that this signified that the priest should dedicate all his faculties and powers to the service of God, the ear that he might hear God's word, the hand that he might do his work, the foot that he might walk in his ways; that the whole man, sanctified by blood, was claimed for God, what he hears, does, and walks; the type, that the blood of Christ sanctifies the whole man, ear, hand, and foot, the believer wholly given up to God).

debates.question: What does the blood on the right ear, thumb, and great toe signify?

sides.position: That the priest should dedicate all his faculties and powers to God's service — the ear to hear his word, the hand to do his work, the foot to walk in his ways; the whole man, sanctified by blood, claimed for God, a type of the blood of Christ sanctifying the whole man

themes: Moses took of the blood of it, and put it upon the tip of Aaron's right ear, and upon the thumb of his right hand
and upon the great toe of his right foot
the blood put on Aaron's right ear, thumb, and great toe, dedicating all his faculties to God
the ear to hear, the hand to do, the foot to walk; the whole man sanctified by blood, a type of the blood of Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: having performed these symbolical acts upon the high priest, Moses now repeats the same in the case of the sons; the witnesses note Moses bringing Aaron's sons and putting the blood on their right ear, thumb, and toe, as on their father, and sprinkling the blood round the altar — the same consecration of the sons, all their powers given to God, and the blood on the altar (the sons too consecrated in every faculty: 'Moses put of the blood upon the tip of their right ear, and upon the thumbs of their right hands, and upon the great toes of their right feet: and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about'; that the same symbolical acts done on the high priest were repeated in the case of the sons, all the priests alike consecrated; the three places, the ear, thumb, and toe, the same as for Aaron, dedicating all the sons' faculties to God; the sprinkling, 'sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about,' the rest of the blood applied to the altar; the meaning before given, that all God's priests, high and common, are to dedicate all their powers to him, what they hear, do, and walk; the type, that all believers, the spiritual priesthood, are sanctified in every faculty by the blood of Christ).

themes: Moses put of the blood upon the tip of their right ear, and upon the thumbs of their right hands, and upon the great toes of their right feet
and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about
the same blood put on the sons' ear, thumb, and toe as on their father, the blood sprinkled on the altar
a type that all believers, the spiritual priesthood, are sanctified in every faculty by the blood of Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'he took the fat, and the rump,' better, the fat and the fat-tail, the rich portions of the ram of consecration; the witnesses note Moses taking the fat, the rump, the inward fat, the caul, the kidneys, and the right shoulder of the ram of consecration — the choice parts of the ram of consecration set apart for the rite (the best, with the right shoulder, taken for the filling of the hands: 'he took the fat, and the rump... and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and their fat, and the right shoulder'; the parts, 'the fat, and the rump... the caul... the two kidneys... and the right shoulder,' the rich fat, the fat-tail, the inward fat, and the right shoulder, all the choicest portions; the difference, that here, unlike the other offerings, the right shoulder too was taken, the priest's portion; the reason, that, being the consecration sacrifice, its choice parts were used to fill the priests' hands; that the best and choicest of the offering was given to this special rite; that God claims the best in the consecration of his servants).

themes: he took the fat, and the rump, and all the fat that was upon the inwards, and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and their fat
and the right shoulder
the choicest parts of the ram of consecration taken for the rite, to fill the priests' hands
here the right shoulder too taken, the priest's portion; God claiming the best in the consecration of his servants

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was ordered to take out of the basket of unleavened bread one unleavened cake, a cake of oiled bread, and one wafer, and put them on the fat and the right shoulder; the witnesses note Moses taking one cake, one oiled cake, and one wafer from the basket and laying them on the fat and the right shoulder — the joining of the bread to the offering, the meat-offering with the flesh (the bread added to the ram's portions: 'out of the basket of unleavened bread... he took one unleavened cake, and a cake of oiled bread, and one wafer, and put them on the fat, and upon the right shoulder'; the three sorts, 'one unleavened cake... a cake of oiled bread... one wafer,' one of each kind from the basket, taken with the choice parts of the ram; the joining, 'put them on the fat, and upon the right shoulder,' that the bread was laid on the flesh, the meat-offering with the sacrifice; that the bread and the flesh together filled the priests' hands; the type, that the bread, the meat-offering of thanksgiving, with the ram, shadows Christ, the bread of life and the sacrifice, and the thankful devotion of his people; that thanksgiving accompanies the offering).

themes: out of the basket of unleavened bread... he took one unleavened cake, and a cake of oiled bread, and one wafer
and put them on the fat, and upon the right shoulder
one of each bread joined to the ram's choice parts, the meat-offering with the flesh
a type of Christ the bread of life and the sacrifice, and the thankful devotion of his people

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the fat and the right shoulder, with the cakes upon them, were put into their hands and waved as a wave offering before the LORD; the witnesses note Moses putting all the portions in the hands of Aaron and his sons and waving them before the LORD — the filling of the priests' hands, the wave offering, the very act of consecration (their hands filled and the offering waved: 'he put all upon Aaron's hands, and upon his sons' hands, and waved them for a wave offering before the LORD'; the filling, 'put all upon Aaron's hands, and... his sons' hands,' that the choice parts of the ram and the bread were put into their hands, the very mark of consecration, filling the hand; the waving, 'waved them for a wave offering before the LORD,' that they, with the offerings in their hands, waved them to and fro before the LORD, presenting them to God; the meaning the commentators draw, that the filling of the hands signified the giving to them of their priestly office and its service, their hands now full of the things of God; the type, that Christ fills his people's hands with himself and his gifts, consecrating them to their priesthood; that the consecrated hand is a hand filled by God).

debates.question: What does 'filling the hand' (the wave offering put in the priests' hands) signify?

sides.position: The very act of consecration — whence in the Hebrew 'to consecrate' is 'to fill the hand'; the offerings put into their hands and waved before the LORD denote their investiture with their office, a type of Christ filling his people's hands with himself and his gifts

themes: he put all upon Aaron's hands, and upon his sons' hands
and waved them for a wave offering before the LORD
the offerings put in the priests' hands and waved, the very act of consecration ('filling the hand')
a type of Christ filling his people's hands with himself and his gifts, consecrating them to their priesthood

schools.summary: Gill: Moses took them from off their hands, after they had been waved before the Lord, and burnt them on the altar upon the burnt offering; the witnesses note Moses taking the offerings from the priests' hands and burning them on the altar upon the burnt offering, consecrations for a sweet savour to the LORD — the burning of the wave offering, the consecration completed in fire (what was waved now burnt to God: 'Moses took them from off their hands, and burnt them on the altar upon the burnt offering: they were consecrations for a sweet savour'; the receiving, 'took them from off their hands,' that Moses, the priests not yet able to present it themselves, took the offerings from their filled hands after they had been waved; the burning, 'burnt them on the altar upon the burnt offering,' that the waved portions were burnt upon the burnt offering, the two going up together; the name and acceptance, 'they were consecrations for a sweet savour,' that this was the consecration offering, well-pleasing and accepted; that the consecration, having filled their hands and been waved, was at last consumed in fire to God; the type, that all our consecration ends in being wholly given up to God, a sweet savour through Christ).

themes: Moses took them from off their hands, and burnt them on the altar upon the burnt offering
they were consecrations for a sweet savour
the offerings taken from the priests' hands and burnt on the altar, the consecration completed in fire
all our consecration ending in being wholly given up to God, a sweet savour through Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Moses took the breast, that is, the breast-piece, which was the portion of the officiating priest, here Moses; the witnesses note Moses taking the breast of the ram of consecration and waving it before the LORD, for it was his part, as the LORD commanded — the breast given to Moses as the officiating priest, the portion of him that served (the wave breast Moses' part: 'Moses took the breast, and waved it for a wave offering before the LORD: for of the ram of consecration it was Moses' part'; the breast, 'the breast,' the breast-piece, which was the portion of the officiating priest, here Moses, who served as priest in this rite; the waving, 'waved it for a wave offering before the LORD,' presenting it to God and receiving it back; the portion, 'of the ram of consecration it was Moses' part,' that the breast fell to Moses, who officiated, as his due; the refrain, 'as the LORD commanded Moses,' exact obedience; the order, that God provides a portion for him that serves at the altar, they who serve God being fed from his altar; that God gives his ministers their portion).

themes: Moses took the breast, and waved it for a wave offering before the LORD: for of the ram of consecration it was Moses' part
as the LORD commanded Moses
the breast of the ram of consecration given to Moses, the officiating priest, as his part
God providing a portion for him that serves at the altar, they who serve God fed from his altar

schools.summary: Gill: Moses took of the anointing oil and of the blood which was upon the altar, and these two seem to be mingled together, and sprinkled them upon Aaron and his sons and their garments; the witnesses note Moses taking the anointing oil and the blood from the altar and sprinkling them on Aaron, his sons, and their garments, sanctifying them — the joining of blood and oil, the hallowing of the priests by atonement and the Spirit (blood and oil together sanctifying them: 'Moses took of the anointing oil, and of the blood which was upon the altar, and sprinkled it upon Aaron, and upon his garments... and sanctified Aaron, and his garments, and his sons'; the two together, 'the anointing oil, and... the blood,' that the atoning blood and the anointing oil, mingled together, were sprinkled on the priests; the sprinkling, 'upon Aaron, and upon his garments, and upon his sons,' both the persons and the garments; the result, 'sanctified Aaron, and his garments, and his sons,' that all were thereby consecrated, made holy; the great meaning the commentators draw, that the blood and the oil together shadow the two great means of consecration, the blood of Christ, atonement, and the Spirit, anointing, by which alone God's people and their service are hallowed; that we are sanctified by blood and Spirit).

debates.question: What do the blood and the anointing oil sprinkled together signify?

sides.position: The two great means of consecration — the blood of Christ (atonement) and the Spirit (anointing) — by which alone God's people and their service are hallowed; we are sanctified by blood and Spirit

themes: Moses took of the anointing oil, and of the blood which was upon the altar, and sprinkled it upon Aaron, and upon his garments, and upon his sons
and sanctified Aaron, and his garments, and his sons, and his sons' garments with him
the atoning blood and the anointing oil sprinkled together on the priests and garments, to sanctify them
the two great means of consecration, the blood of Christ and the Spirit, by which God's people are sanctified

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the priests were to eat the flesh of the ram and the bread, to signify that God not only called them but fed them at his table; the witnesses note Moses bidding Aaron and his sons boil the flesh of the ram and eat it with the bread at the door, as commanded — the holy meal of the priests, feeding on the consecration offering (the priests eating of their offering before God: 'Boil the flesh at the door of the tabernacle... and there eat it with the bread that is in the basket of consecrations... Aaron and his sons shall eat it'; the boiling, 'boil the flesh at the door,' that the flesh of the ram of consecration was cooked at the door of the tabernacle; the eating, 'there eat it with the bread that is in the basket of consecrations,' that the priests fed on the consecration flesh and bread, the holy meal; the meaning Henry draws, that the priests were to eat to signify that God not only called them but fed them at his table; the type, that the priests' eating of the consecration flesh shadows the feeding on Christ, the believer's communion with God at his table; that God feeds those he consecrates to himself).

themes: Boil the flesh at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation: and there eat it with the bread that is in the basket of consecrations
Aaron and his sons shall eat it
the priests boiling and eating the flesh of the ram with the bread at the door, the holy meal
God not only called them but fed them at his table, a type of feeding on Christ, who feeds those he consecrates

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: that which remained of the flesh and the bread until the morning was to be burnt with fire, not eaten; the witnesses note God appointing that any flesh or bread of the consecration left over be burnt with fire — the burning of the holy remnant, that nothing holy be left to corruption or common use (the leftover holy things burnt: 'that which remaineth of the flesh and of the bread shall ye burn with fire'; the remnant, 'that which remaineth of the flesh and of the bread,' what was left, until the next morning, which could not be eaten by Aaron and his sons; the command, 'burn with fire,' that the leftover was not to be kept or eaten on the morrow, but consumed with fire; the reason before given, that this consecration food, being most holy, must not be left to grow stale or be put to common use, but burnt; that holy things are to be treated as holy, nothing of God's portion left to corruption; that God will have his holy things kept holy).

themes: And that which remaineth of the flesh and of the bread
shall ye burn with fire
the remnant of the consecration flesh and bread burnt with fire, not kept to the morrow, because most holy
holy things to be treated as holy, nothing of God's portion left to corruption or common use

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to go out of the door of the tabernacle in seven days, which was the time of their consecration, so long it lasted; the witnesses note God charging the priests not to go out of the door of the tabernacle for seven days, until their consecration was ended, for seven days he would consecrate them — the seven days' continuance at the tabernacle, the full and solemn consecration (the priests abiding seven days till the rite is complete: 'ye shall not go out of the door of the tabernacle... in seven days, until the days of your consecration be at an end: for seven days shall he consecrate you'; the charge, 'not go out... in seven days,' that they were to keep by the tabernacle, not leaving it, through the whole time; the term, 'until the days of your consecration be at an end,' that the consecration lasted seven days, the perfect number; that the rite was not done in a day but continued seven days, a full and complete consecration; the meaning, that God would have his servants thoroughly and solemnly consecrated, kept apart with him the whole time; that the seven days, the perfect number, marked a complete setting apart; that God's servants are to be wholly given to him, abiding in his presence).

themes: ye shall not go out of the door of the tabernacle of the congregation in seven days, until the days of your consecration be at an end
for seven days shall he consecrate you
the priests charged not to leave the tabernacle for seven days, until their consecration was ended
the seven days marking a full and complete consecration, God's servants thoroughly consecrated, kept apart with him

schools.summary: Gill: the same things were to be repeated every day until the seven days were ended, so Jarchi and Aben Ezra, to make atonement for the priests; the witnesses note God appointing that what was done that day should be done each of the seven days, to make atonement for them — the daily repeating of the rite through the seven days, the atonement renewed (the consecration renewed each day to make atonement: 'As he hath done this day, so the LORD hath commanded to do, to make an atonement for you'; the repeating, 'as he hath done this day, so... to do,' that the same were to be repeated every day until the seven days were ended; the end, 'to make an atonement for you,' that the daily rite, especially the daily sin offering, made atonement for the priests each day of the consecration; the meaning the commentators draw, that the priests needed atonement renewed, being men of infirmity, and that the daily repeating marked the fulness of the atonement; the contrast with Christ, who, having no sin of his own, needed not to offer daily, but made atonement once for all; that our atonement is sure and complete in Christ).

debates.question: How does the daily-repeated atonement of the priests contrast with Christ?

sides.position: The priests, being men of infirmity, needed atonement renewed each of the seven days; but Christ, having no sin of his own, needed not to offer daily but made atonement once for all (Hebrews 7:27), our atonement sure and complete in him

themes: As he hath done this day, so the LORD hath commanded to do
to make an atonement for you
the rite repeated each of the seven days, to make atonement for the priests
the daily atonement renewed because they were men of infirmity; a contrast with Christ, who made atonement once for all

schools.summary: Gill: they were to abide, or rather within the door, since they were not to go out, day and night for seven days, and keep the charge of the LORD, that they die not; the witnesses note God charging the priests to abide at the door day and night seven days, keeping the charge of the LORD, that they die not — the strict charge of continual attendance and obedience, on pain of death (they keep the LORD's charge and abide, lest they die: 'ye shall abide at the door of the tabernacle... day and night seven days, and keep the charge of the LORD, that ye die not'; the abiding, 'abide at the door... day and night seven days,' that they were to keep within the tabernacle, by the door, the whole time, not leaving it; the charge, 'keep the charge of the LORD,' that they were to observe carefully all God required of them in this solemn time; the solemn sanction, 'that ye die not,' that to fail in the charge would be death, as Nadab and Abihu soon proved; the warrant, 'for so I am commanded,' that Moses laid all on God's command; the meaning the commentators draw, that the nearer one is brought to God in holy service, the more solemn the charge to obey exactly, and the more dangerous to fail; that God will be sanctified in them that come near him).

themes: ye shall abide at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation day and night seven days, and keep the charge of the LORD, that ye die not
for so I am commanded
the priests charged to abide at the door seven days and keep the LORD's charge, on pain of death
the nearer one is brought to God in holy service, the more solemn the charge to obey exactly; God sanctified in them that come near

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron and his sons submitted to have done to them and for them all that was done on this occasion, and did all things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses; the witnesses note Aaron and his sons doing all things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses — the close of the consecration, the obedience of the priests, all done as God commanded (they submitted and obeyed in all: 'Aaron and his sons did all things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses'; the obedience, 'did all things which the LORD commanded,' that they submitted to have done to them, and for them, and themselves did all that was required; the channel, 'by the hand of Moses,' that God's commands came to them through Moses, his servant and minister; the refrain of the chapter, that all was done as the LORD commanded, from first to last; the meaning the commentators draw, that the priests' willing obedience and submission is the fit close of their consecration, the consecrated heart yielding wholly to God's will; that those whom God consecrates to himself obey him in all; that the way to be established in God's service is full obedience to his word).

themes: So Aaron and his sons did all things
which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses
Aaron and his sons doing all things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses, the close of the consecration
the consecrated heart yielding wholly to God's will; those whom God consecrates obey him in all, full obedience the way to be established in his service
